issue: Order can be marked paid with fully client-supplied, unverified payment data (no PayPal capture verification)
evidence: order.isPaid = true; order.paymentResult = { id: req.body.id, status: req.body.status, ... email_address: req.body.payer.email_address }
owasp: A04 Insecure Design / A08 Data Integrity Failures
recommendation: Verify the payment server-side against PayPal (orders/{id}/capture or webhook) before setting isPaid. Compare captured amount to order.totalPrice and ensure the transaction id has not already been used. Never trust the client to declare payment status.

issue: IDOR — getOrderById returns any order by id with no ownership/admin check; any authenticated user can read other users' orders (incl. shipping address, email)
evidence: const order = await Order.findById(req.params.id).populate('user', 'name email'); ... res.json(order)  // route: router.route('/:id').get(protect, getOrderById)
recommendation: After fetch, enforce: if (!order.user.equals(req.user._id) && !req.user.isAdmin) { res.status(403); throw new Error('Not authorized') }.

issue: IDOR on pay endpoint — updateOrderToPaid is protect-only and never checks the order belongs to req.user; any logged-in user can mark another user's order paid
evidence: router.route('/:id/pay').put(protect, updateOrderToPaid)  // controller does Order.findById(req.params.id) with no owner check
recommendation: Verify order.user.equals(req.user._id) (or admin) before mutating, and combine with server-side PayPal verification (SEC-01).

issue: Admin list-users endpoint leaks bcrypt password hashes and full user docs (missing .select('-password'))
evidence: const users = await User.find({})  // no field projection; returns hashed password field
recommendation: Use User.find({}).select('-password'). Hashes should never leave the server; exposing them enables offline cracking if the admin token/response is captured.

issue: NoSQL regex injection / ReDoS — user-supplied keyword is passed unescaped into a MongoDB $regex with case-insensitive option
recommendation: Escape regex metacharacters before building the query, or use a MongoDB text index / $text search. Crafted patterns (e.g. nested quantifiers) can cause catastrophic backtracking (ReDoS) and unbounded CPU. Cross-ref performance-mate (DoS).

issue: No rate-limiting / brute-force protection on login or registration
evidence: router.post('/login', authUser)  // and router.route('/').post(registerUser) — no express-rate-limit anywhere in the app
recommendation: Add express-rate-limit (e.g. 5-10 attempts / 15 min, keyed by IP+email) on /login and /users registration. No rate-limit dependency exists in package.json.

issue: No server-side password strength / input validation on registration or profile update (empty or 1-char passwords accepted)
evidence: const user = await User.create({ name, email, password })  // password used verbatim, no length/complexity/format checks
recommendation: Validate inputs server-side (e.g. Joi/express-validator): enforce min password length, email format, name limits. Frontend validation is not a control.

issue: Upload filename built from user-controlled originalname extension; MIME/extension check is weak (substring regex) and there is no file-size limit
evidence: filename: `${file.fieldname}-${Date.now()}${path.extname(file.originalname)}`  // filetypes.test(...) is unanchored; no limits:{fileSize}
owasp: A03 Injection / A08 Data Integrity Failures
recommendation: Anchor the regex (e.g. /\.(jpe?g|png)$/), validate the decoded extension against an allowlist, generate the stored filename server-side (uuid + fixed ext), and set multer limits.fileSize to bound DoS via large uploads.

issue: Feature-flags read API is fully public; entire flag configuration (testing features, traffic %, dependencies) is exposed to anonymous users
evidence: router.route('/').get(getFeatureFlags)  // no protect/admin; comment even says 'admin-only consumed by Dashboard'
recommendation: Gate GET /api/feature-flags and /:name with protect+admin (matching the documented intent), or strip sensitive fields for the public response. Leaking flag internals aids reconnaissance of unreleased features.

issue: Feature-flags MCP can be exposed over HTTP/SSE bound to 0.0.0.0 with NO authentication, exposing unauthenticated write tools (set_feature_state / adjust_traffic_rollout) on the network
evidence: mcp.run(transport=args.transport, host=args.host, port=args.port)  // host default 127.0.0.1 but docs instruct --host 0.0.0.0 for n8n; no auth on tools
recommendation: When binding non-loopback, require an auth token/header on the MCP transport (or front it with an authenticating reverse proxy / network policy). Anyone reaching the port can flip production feature flags.

issue: n8n API key shipped to the browser — REACT_APP_N8N_API_KEY is inlined into the public CRA bundle and sent as X-API-Key from client-side fetch
evidence: const N8N_API_KEY = process.env.REACT_APP_N8N_API_KEY ... headers: { ...(N8N_API_KEY ? { 'X-API-Key': N8N_API_KEY } : {}) }  // on-disk frontend/.env holds a real 64-hex key
recommendation: Never put secrets in REACT_APP_* — all such vars are public in the built JS. Proxy the n8n webhook through the backend, which holds the key server-side. Rotate the current key (it is in the local build pipeline). The code comment acknowledges this is a simplification — flag for production hardening.

issue: Multiple critical/high dependency CVEs in backend stack (npm audit: 2 critical, 10 high)
evidence: mongoose@^5.10.6 (critical, search-injection / prototype pollution in 5.x), jsonwebtoken@^8.5.1 (high — alg/JWT verification CVEs), multer@^1.4.2 (high — busboy/dicer DoS), minimist (critical), express@^4.17.1 (high). `npm audit`: 20 vulns (5 low, 3 moderate, 10 high, 2 critical)
recommendation: Upgrade jsonwebtoken to >=9, mongoose to a patched 5.13.x+ (or 6/7), multer to >=1.4.4-lts/2.x, and run npm audit fix. Note CLAUDE.md pins old versions intentionally — at minimum patch jsonwebtoken (auth-critical) and mongoose (injection).

issue: JWT_SECRET appears to be very short (~10 chars), weakening HMAC-signed JWT against brute-force / dictionary attacks
evidence: JWT_SECRET=<~10-char value> (line length 21 bytes incl. 'JWT_SECRET='); file is correctly gitignored and NOT tracked
recommendation: Use a high-entropy secret >=32 bytes (openssl rand -hex 32). A short HS256 secret can be brute-forced offline once a token is captured, allowing forgery of admin tokens. (.env itself is not committed — good.)

issue: Admin status (isAdmin) is read from JWT claims/DB but the JWT carries no isAdmin freshness; ADR-003 documents demoted-admin retains privileges up to 30d. Token never expires server-side (no revocation)
evidence: const admin = (req, res, next) => { if (req.user && req.user.isAdmin) next() ... }  // req.user comes from DB so isAdmin is fresh, but token validity (30d) cannot be revoked
recommendation: Documented/accepted in ADR-003 (technical debt). For hardening: shorter access-token TTL + refresh tokens, or a token denylist / tokenVersion check. NOTE: protect() does re-fetch the user from DB so isAdmin is actually current — the stale-claim risk is lower than ADR implies.

issue: JWT stored in localStorage (XSS-exfiltratable) — accepted ADR-003 tradeoff, recorded for completeness
evidence: const userInfoFromStorage = localStorage.getItem('userInfo') ? JSON.parse(...) : null  // token persisted in localStorage
recommendation: ADR-003 accepts this. Hardening path: HttpOnly+Secure+SameSite cookie for the token so JS cannot read it. Mitigate XSS aggressively in the meantime (no dangerouslySetInnerHTML found — good).

issue: Raw error object (incl. stack) logged to console on every auth failure; no structured/audit logging for security events (failed logins, admin actions)
recommendation: Log a sanitized message for auth failures and add audit logging for security-relevant events (failed login attempts, privilege changes, order payment status changes). Avoid dumping full error objects in production.

issue: No security headers / hardening middleware (helmet) and no CORS policy; relies on CRA dev proxy. body parser has no size limit
evidence: app.use(express.json())  // no helmet(), no express.json({ limit }), no cors config; package.json has no helmet dependency
recommendation: Add helmet() for baseline headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, frame options, CSP), set express.json({ limit: '100kb' }) to bound JSON-body DoS, and define an explicit CORS allowlist for production.

issue: Admin updateUser sets isAdmin directly from req.body without validation; combined with no audit log, enables silent privilege escalation if an admin account is compromised or a mass-assignment slips in
evidence: user.isAdmin = req.body.isAdmin  // assigns whatever the client sends, including undefined -> could unset required field
recommendation: Validate isAdmin is a boolean, audit-log admin/role changes, and guard against an admin demoting/locking out the last admin. Minor, but defense-in-depth around privilege management.

issue: User-controlled feature name reflected into error message; low risk in JSON API but unbounded key lookup with no allowlist
evidence: throw new Error(`Feature '${req.params.name}' not found`)  // req.params.name echoed; res.json so not HTML-injected
recommendation: Low priority (JSON response, no HTML context). Consider not reflecting raw input in errors as a hygiene measure.
